channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Defends Deal to End the War With Iran as Details Emerge
item.description: President Trump lashed out at critics who say the agreement achieves less than the one President Barack Obama signed in 2015, and he threatened to bomb Iran again if it violated the deal.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green, Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Farnaz Fassihi and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump and other U.S. officials at a news conference amid the Group of 7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, on Wednesday.

item.title: U.S.-Iran Agreement Includes Strait of Hormuz, Lebanon and More: See Full Text of the Deal
item.description: New York Times reporters annotated the agreement, which a senior official disclosed on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament

item.title: Trump Criticized Obama’s Iran Deal, but New Deal Would Also Offer Tehran Relief
item.description: The agreement outlines a $300 billion plan to rebuild Iran, and says sanctions would be lifted in the future.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump speaks on Iran during the closing of the G7 Summit in Evian-les-Bains, France.

item.title: Trump Escalates Tensions With Senate Republicans
item.description: Also, the U.S. shares details of the preliminary Iran deal. Here’s the latest at the end of Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen

item.title: Trump Feuds With Thune and G.O.P., Stoking Election-Year Rift
item.description: The president upended the majority leader’s plans by yanking his intelligence nominee from a confirmation hearing and insisting on an end to the filibuster.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, Republican of South Dakota and the majority leader, has shown more willingness in recent days to push back against President Trump no matter the fallout.

item.title: Bill Pulte, a Bulldog for Trump, Prepares to Take Reins at Spy Agency
item.description: Democrats and some Republicans excoriated President Trump’s pick for a top national security job. The president pushed ahead anyway.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: Bill Pulte has used his current post as a top federal housing official to help with President Trump’s campaign of retribution against his perceived enemies.

item.title: Trump Administration to Pay $765 Million to Cancel 4 More Wind Projects
item.description: It’s the third such deal the Interior Department has struck to pay firms to abandon plans for offshore turbines, spending roughly $2.5 billion to get companies to abandon their wind projects.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:description: The Trump administration has now struck deals energy developers totaling more than $2.5 billion to cancel offshore wind leases.

item.title: France Assembled an African Dream Team. Senegal Could Not Stop Them.
item.description: Many see the rivalry between France and Senegal as a result of colonial heritage. But at the same time, European teams are more African than ever.
item.dc:creator: Saikou Jammeh
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: Eight out of the 11 France players, in blue, who started the match against Senegal on Tuesday, were first- or second-generation immigrants, including Michael Olise, left, 24, born in the U.K. to a Nigerian father and French-Algerian mother.

item.title: Fed Meeting Live Updates: Kevin Warsh to Address U.S. Economy as Fed Holds Interest Rates Steady
item.description: Officials at the Federal Reserve were split between no cuts this year and one or more rate increases as they braced for higher inflation, according to a new set of projections.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: See How the First Fed Statement Under Warsh Evolved
item.description: Interest rates didn’t change, but the language in Fed’s policy statement, which it released alongside its rate decision today, certainly did.
item.dc:creator: Christine Zhang

item.title: Georgia Republicans Shelve Redistricting Amid Mounting Protests
item.description: The leaders of the Georgia legislature pulled the plug on gerrymandering away U.S. House seats held by Black Democrats just hours before a special session was to begin.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas, Sean Keenan and Alessandro Marazzi Sassoon
item.media:description: Protesters gathered at the Georgia state capitol in Atlanta Wednesday, determined to stop the legislature from redistricting the state.

item.title: Susan Collins Laments Roe’s Fall but Doesn’t ‘Regret’ Confirming Kavanaugh
item.description: Ms. Collins, a Republican in Maine facing a tough re-election battle, defended her vote to confirm Justice Brett M. Kavanaugh as Democrats look to capitalize on it politically.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Susan Collins of Maine is one of only two Republican senators who support abortion rights.

item.title: Trump Rescinds Endorsement of Right-Wing Pastor After Texting Scandal
item.description: The pastor, Jackson Lahmeyer, dropped out of the race for a House seat in Oklahoma as President Trump backed Mr. Lahmeyer’s Republican rival in a runoff election.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Michael Noble Jr. for The New York Times; Tedford for U.S. Congress
item.media:description: Jackson Lahmeyer, left, and Mark Tedford had advanced to a Republican primary runoff election on Tuesday, but Mr. Lahmeyer ended his bid on Wednesday.

item.title: New Plan Scales Back C.D.C.’s Work on Diseases Abroad
item.description: The State Department is taking over much of the control of global health initiatives, for which critics say the department does not have the expertise.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:description: Demonstrators in Washington in 2025 protest the withholding of funding from the President’s Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief, PEPFAR.

item.title: Elon Musk’s Next Move May Be a Mega-Merger of SpaceX and Tesla
item.description: Some shareholders might object, but there is little they could do, legal experts say.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Tesla has sold car and batteries to SpaceX, and the two companies are jointly working on chips and software.

item.title: Why Waymo’s Driverless Taxis Won’t Be on Your Streets Anytime Soon
item.description: The Silicon Valley company’s ambitions to roll out autonomous cars nationwide have hit political roadblocks in some of the country’s biggest markets.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times

item.title: Shane Hennen, the One Man Linking 3 Gambling Scandals, Is Ready to Talk
item.description: The N.B.A. and the N.C.A.A. have been rocked by indictments accusing players and others of fixing bets and playing in rigged poker games. Shane Hennen is named in each one.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar and Tania Ganguli
item.media:credit: Ryan Pfluger for The New York Times

item.title: A Tournament on Native Land Makes Millions. A Tribe Wants a Bigger Cut.
item.description: The Shinnecock Hills Golf Club on Long Island earns millions when it hosts the U.S. Open. But the nearby Indian tribe has for years only received a fraction of that.
item.dc:creator: Corey Kilgannon
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lisa Goree, the chairwoman of the Shinnecock Indian Nation, why says the golf course hosting the U.S. Open this week on Long Island was built on their land.

item.title: How Ecuador’s Andes Created a Language of Its Own
item.description: In Ecuador’s highlands, a seamless mix of Kichwa and Spanish creates a language that bends grammar, adds melody and goes unnoticed by many who speak it every day.
item.dc:creator: José María León Cabrera and Johanna Alarcón
item.media:credit: Johanna Alarcon for The New York Times
item.media:description: San Roque market in Quito, the capital of Ecuador, is one of the main meeting points between Kichwa and Spanish. These languages have long intertwined through exchanges in the city’s markets and public squares.

item.title: Gilgo Beach Serial Killer Receives Life Sentence After Guilty Plea
item.description: The decision on Wednesday brought an end an infamous case that took investigators more than a decade to solve.
item.dc:creator: Corey Kilgannon
item.media:description: Rex Heuermann gave only a hushed statement before he was sentenced to life in prison for killing eight women.

item.title: N.Y.P.D. Will Assign Largest Number of Officers Ever to Knicks Parade
item.description: The department announced that it would deploy more than 10,000 of its ranks and put stringent security measures in place for the celebration of the Knicks’ N.B.A. championship on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Maia Coleman
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Police Department said it would deploy more officers for the parade route honoring the Knicks than it had for other large-scale events, including New Year’s Eve.

item.title: What to Know if You’re Going to the Knicks Parade
item.description: New Yorkers will descend on Lower Manhattan on Thursday morning to celebrate the Knicks. Here’s what you should know if you are one of them.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan and Sarah Maslin Nir
item.media:credit: Mimi d’Autremont for The New York Times
item.media:description: The parade starts at 10 a.m., but you’ll want to get there at least two hours earlier.

item.title: Tropical Storm Arthur Forecast: How Much Rain Will Fall?
item.description: Heavy rain and flash flooding are expected across the region through Friday.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:description: Floodwaters rose in Galveston, Texas, on Wednesday.

item.title: Midwest Faces Tornadoes and Strong Winds on Wednesday
item.description: A county in Iowa reported a wind gust of 94 miles per hour during early morning storms. More are expected into the night.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar

item.title: The Democrats Need a Candidate Shake-Up
item.description: The 2024 election showed that the party is simply not large enough to command a majority of the country.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Yglesias
item.media:credit: Thalassa Raasch for The New York Times

item.title: The Trump U.F.C. Fights Helped Me Understand My Sons
item.description: In my darker moments, I worry I’ve failed them.
item.dc:creator: Hope Reeves
item.media:credit: Mark Peterson for The New York Times

item.title: Man Dies in Fall From Horse Carriage in Central Park
item.description: The horse bolted after the driver stepped out to take a photo of the passengers, the carriage drivers’ union said, and an 18-year-old tourist from India fell from the driverless carriage.
item.dc:creator: Andy Newman, Miles G. Cohen and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: The accident was the latest in a series of mishaps involving horse carriages.
